claim: A two-camera array increases survey detection probability by an average of 80% (range 40–128%) compared to a single camera across four wildlife species — the single biggest gain in the entire 1–10 camera range.
stance_target: two-camera minimum as the cost-effective standard for lost dog feeding station surveillance
scope_conditions: deer, bobcat, raccoon, opossum in Connecticut; non-baited cameras; 5,000 Monte Carlo iterations; year-long dataset
quoted_support: The use of a two camera array increased survey detection an average of 80% (range 40–128%) from the detection probability of a single camera across the four species.

claim: For cryptic/infrequent species (bobcat, opossum), a single camera at maximum season length (180 days) still achieved only 0.13–0.14 detection probability — no amount of time compensates for using a single camera for low-frequency subjects.
stance_target: single-camera deployment as inadequate for detecting shy/fleeing lost dogs regardless of how long it is deployed
scope_conditions: cryptic species with naturally low detection probability; analogous to traumatized fleeing dogs that visit infrequently
quoted_support: for two of our modeled species, Virginia opossum and bobcat, data from a single camera produced maximum season-long detectabilities of 0.13 and 0.14, respectively, even after 180 days. The addition of a second, randomly-placed camera to create an array increased season detectability over a 180-day survey to 0.86 and 0.54, respectively.

claim: The maximum array size at which adding cameras no longer significantly improved detection was 8–9 cameras for all four species — for field operations, 2–3 cameras at a feeding station captures the majority of available gain.
stance_target: 2–3 camera arrays as the practical optimum for lost dog feeding station monitoring
scope_conditions: non-baited cameras at 1-ha sites; real-world lost dog stations are baited (higher base detection), so diminishing returns kick in earlier
quoted_support: The array size where adding additional cameras to the array no longer significantly improved survey detectability, was between eight and nine cameras for all four species.

claim: Longer deployment with a single camera does not compensate for camera array size — season-long detectability asymptotes below 0.15 for cryptic species even after extended periods, regardless of season length.
stance_target: array size (multiple cameras) as the primary lever for detection improvement, not deployment duration
scope_conditions: cryptic/infrequent species; multi-season deployment; Monte Carlo simulation
quoted_support: increasing season length did not always result in meaningful increases in detectability, and that where increases occurred, they were not equal to those achieved by increasing array size. While season-long detectability curves for our simulated arrays sometimes reached asymptotes quickly (usually around a 100-day season), for single cameras, these asymptotes were <0.15 for our two focal species with relatively low probabilities of detection.
